summary: A hanif (Arabic ḥanīf) is, in Qur'anic and Islamic usage, the pure, primordial monotheist who turns away from idolatry to the worship of the one God, pre-eminently Abraham. The Qur'an describes Abraham as 'neither a Jew nor a Christian, but a hanif, a muslim' (Q 3:67), and academic scholarship has extensively discussed the term's uncertain pre-Islamic background and etymology.
description: In the Qur'an the term ḥanīf (pl. ḥunafāʾ) designates one who inclines away from idolatry and false worship toward the exclusive worship of the one God, a pure and upright monotheist. It is applied above all to Abraham: the Qur'an states that 'Abraham was neither a Jew nor a Christian, but he was a hanif, a muslim, and he was not of the idolaters' (Q 3:67), enjoins following 'the religion of Abraham, a hanif' (Q 2:135, cf. Q 3:95), and depicts Abraham turning his face away from the worship of star, moon, and sun to God who created the heavens and the earth, 'a hanif' (Q 6:79, cf. Q 6:161, 16:120-123). The term is closely tied to the fitra, the sound primordial nature: Q 30:30 commands setting one's face 'toward the religion as a hanif, the fitra of God upon which He created mankind.' In Islamic self-understanding, then, the hanif embodies the original, uncorrupted monotheism that precedes and underlies the later communities, and Islam presents itself as the restoration of this primordial hanifiyya. Modern academic scholarship has extensively discussed the pre-Islamic background and etymology of the term, and the discussion remains unresolved: the Arabic ḥanīf is widely connected to a Syriac cognate (ḥanpā) that carried the sense of 'pagan' or 'heathen', which appears to invert its Qur'anic sense of pure monotheist, and scholars have debated whether a group of pre-Islamic Arabian ḥunafāʾ (monotheists dissatisfied with idolatry, associated in the Islamic sources with the Ka'ba and the 'religion of Abraham', dīn Ibrāhīm) existed historically or is a construction of later tradition. Uri Rubin has examined the pre-Islamic Arabian background of this ḥanīfiyya and its link to the Ka'ba, while G. R. Hawting has argued more broadly that the Qur'anic polemic against idolatry may be directed at monotheists accused of compromising strict monotheism rather than literal pagans, a reading that bears on how the hanif ideal is understood. This entry attributes the academic debate without resolving it. The hanif ideal connects directly to the wider Qur'anic theme of the religion of Abraham (millat Ibrahim), to the oneness of God (tawhid) that the hanif affirms, to the fitra it expresses, and stands opposed to shirk, the association of partners with God.
editorial_notes: This entry states the Qur'anic and Islamic understanding of the hanif fully on its own terms (neutrality #6, #7). The load-bearing claim that touches other traditions is Q 3:67, that Abraham was 'neither a Jew nor a Christian, but a hanif, a muslim.' Per the #7-and-#11 balance this is presented explicitly as the ATTRIBUTED ISLAMIC/QUR'ANIC VIEW ('the Qur'an states', 'in Islamic self-understanding') and never as a neutral fact about Abraham nor as a claim that Judaism or Christianity accepts; the entry reports the Qur'anic characterization without adjudicating whether Abraham is rightly so described, and without ranking it against Jewish or Christian understandings of Abraham. Per the instructions the scholarly debate over the term's pre-Islamic background and etymology (the Syriac ḥanpā cognate meaning 'pagan', the historicity of pre-Islamic ḥunafāʾ) is attributed to its proponents and left unresolved, not decided by the editor. Sourcing triangulates three lenses per neutrality #8: a Western academic lens (Uri Rubin's peer-reviewed article on the pre-Islamic background of the ḥanīfiyya, and G. R. Hawting's monograph on the idea of idolatry, the same verified Hawting citation carried in the shirk entry); the primary texts (the Qur'anic ayat and al-Tabari's tafsir); and a contemporary Muslim / Western academic Qur'anic-studies lens (Toshihiko Izutsu's semantic study, treating hanif alongside islām). No encyclopedias or tertiary sources are cited. Confidence is medium throughout: sources verified as extant and on-topic but not read line-by-line; Rubin and Izutsu are new to the corpus and rated medium, Hawting is matched to its existing medium rating in shirk. Relations to millat-ibrahim and islam are forward references authored in the same batch; tawhid, fitra, and shirk exist.

relations.note: The hanif is pre-eminently Abraham; the Qur'an pairs the term with 'the religion of Abraham' (millat Ibrahim), which Islam understands itself to restore. Forward reference authored in the same batch.
The hanif is defined by exclusive worship of the one God; hanifiyya is a mode of the affirmation of tawhid, turning away from idolatry.
Q 30:30 links being a hanif to the fitra, the sound primordial nature upon which God created humankind; the hanif expresses the innate monotheism of the fitra.
The hanif turns away from idolatry and the association of partners with God (shirk); the term is defined in the Qur'an partly by this negation (Q 3:67, 'and he was not of the idolaters').
The Qur'an calls Abraham both a hanif and a muslim (Q 3:67), identifying the pure primordial monotheist with the submitter to God (islām). Forward reference authored in the same batch.

historical_period.note: The term is Qur'anic (revelation from c. 610 CE) and is understood to name a monotheism prior to the later communities; modern scholarship additionally debates a pre-Islamic Arabian background for the ḥunafāʾ and the term's earlier (possibly Syriac) history.

sources.citation: Rubin, Uri. 'Ḥanīfiyya and Kaʿba: An Inquiry into the Arabian Pre-Islamic Background of Dīn Ibrāhīm.' Jerusalem Studies in Arabic and Islam 13 (1990): 85-112 (reprinted in Uri Rubin, Muhammad the Prophet and Arabia, Farnham: Ashgate/Variorum, 2011). A peer-reviewed study of the pre-Islamic Arabian background of the ḥanīfiyya and the 'religion of Abraham', including the association of pre-Islamic ḥunafāʾ (e.g. Zayd b. ʿAmr) with veneration of the Kaʿba. Cited as the Western academic lens on the term's pre-Islamic background. Relevant passages not read line-by-line.
sources.license: Proprietary; cited for reference; relevant passages not read line-by-line.

sources.citation: Hawting, G. R. The Idea of Idolatry and the Emergence of Islam: From Polemic to History. Cambridge Studies in Islamic Civilization. Cambridge: Cambridge University Press, 1999. Academic monograph arguing that the opponents the Qurʾan calls mushrikūn were most likely monotheists accused of compromising strict monotheism rather than literal pagans, a reading that bears on how the hanif ideal and the Qurʾanic polemic against idolatry are understood. Same verified citation as in the shirk entry. Cited for that historical-referent question. Relevant passages not read line-by-line.
sources.license: Proprietary; cited for reference; relevant passages not read line-by-line.

sources.citation: The Qur'an, with al-Ṭabarī, Abū Jaʿfar Muḥammad ibn Jarīr. Jāmiʿ al-bayān ʿan taʾwīl āy al-Qurʾān (classical tafsir). Primary texts: Q 3:67 (Abraham 'neither a Jew nor a Christian, but a hanif, a muslim'), 2:135, 3:95 ('the religion of Abraham, a hanif'), 6:79 and 16:120-123 (Abraham turning from idolatry to God, 'a hanif'), 6:161, and 30:30 (setting one's face toward the religion as a hanif, the fitra of God). Al-Tabari's Jāmiʿ al-bayān is the foundational classical exegesis of these verses. Cited as the primary-text lens.
sources.license: Public-domain classical texts (Qur'an and al-Tabari's tafsir in Arabic; English translations vary).

sources.citation: Izutsu, Toshihiko. Ethico-Religious Concepts in the Qur'an. McGill Islamic Studies, I. Montreal: McGill University Press (for the McGill University Institute of Islamic Studies), 1966. A semantic study of the Qur'an's key ethico-religious terms; it treats the hanif and the vocabulary of pure monotheism alongside islām and īmān within the Qur'anic worldview. Cited as a further Qur'anic-studies lens on the term's Qur'anic sense. Relevant passages not read line-by-line.
sources.license: Proprietary; cited for reference; relevant passages not read line-by-line.
